migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly in 1756
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly in 1756
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

bio.notes: Also spelled Curtiss in some records

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

cross_connections.display_label: Named for him — born same year Brainerd died, 1747

naming_inspiration.description: Named for the Connecticut missionary David Brainerd (X02351, 1718-1747), who died October 9, 1747 — just seven months after Brainard Hooker's birth. Brainerd was Connecticut-born (Haddam) and celebrated for his missionary work when the Hooker baby was named.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War soldier; led family north to Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Brainard Hooker served in the Revolutionary War and moved his family from Wethersfield, Connecticut to Middlebury, Vermont, where he died in October 1808 at age sixty-one. He was survived by his wife Mary Deming Hooker, who outlived him by thirty-five years, dying in 1843 at age ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in 1747 when David Brainerd also died
narrative_blocks.body: Brainard Hooker was born in Woodbury, Connecticut on March 4, 1747 — the same year that the renowned Presbyterian missionary David Brainerd died in Northampton, Massachusetts on October 9, 1747. The name 'Brainard' was rare in colonial Connecticut, but Brainerd was Connecticut-born (Haddam) and celebrated across New England for his missionary work among Delaware Indians; his parents almost certainly named the baby in honor of the dying missionary.

burial.plot_notes: Case Street Cemetery, Middlebury VT per FindAGrave

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Jesse Hooker died in 1791 at approximately age thirty-nine, leaving his wife Sabrina Smith Hooker a widow with six children, the youngest aged five. Sabrina went on to marry John Holmes after Jesse's death.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Maplewood Cemetery Henrietta Monroe County NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived two husbands; died at Henrietta at eighty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker Pixley buried her first husband Isaac Pixley in 1790 when she was thirty-six — left with six children, the youngest barely two — remarried James Root in 1793, bore another child, then buried Root in 1802 when she was forty-eight, and spent the next thirty-nine years a widow in Henrietta, New York, dying on January 6, 1841 at age eighty-seven. She had migrated from Woodbury, Connecticut to the Berkshire hills of Massachusetts as a young wife, and then west again to upstate New York as an old widow, the arc of her life tracking the westward movement of her entire generation — and she outlived every chapter of it, the Puritan inheritance carried through two husbands, seven children, and eighty-seven years.

military_service.notes: EH: 'He served in the Quarter-master's Department in the Revolutionary War.'

narrative_blocks.header: Quartermaster's Department veteran; lived ninety years
narrative_blocks.body: Gilbert Hooker served in the Quartermaster's Department during the Revolutionary War, then settled at Stillwater, New York with his wife Rebecca Andrews where their children were born, later moving the family to Clyde, New York — and lived to see all of it: the nation he helped supply through the Revolution, the expansion across New York State, the rise of his son James Harvey to wealthy merchant at Troy. He died January 9, 1849 at approximately ninety years of age, having outlived his wife Rebecca by eight years — born in Woodbury, Connecticut in 1758, dying when Abraham Lincoln was still an Illinois congressman and the gold rush was just beginning in California, his life spanning the entire arc from Puritan New England colony to young republic.

burial.plot_notes: Maple Grove Cemetery Clyde Wayne County NY per FA
Ward Cemetery, Poultney, Rutland County, Vermont
Congregational Cemetery Castleton Rutland County Vermont per FA

narrative_blocks.header: First two wives both died within two years
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker married Ruth Parmelee in December 1779, and she died September 4, 1781 — less than two years later, shortly after bearing their son Thomas Gould; he then married Ruth Hickok in April 1783, and she died November 16, 1784, barely eighteen months after the wedding. His third wife, Mary 'Polly' Coleman, whom he married at Poultney, Vermont in January 1787, outlived him by twenty years, dying in 1858 at approximately ninety-six — she was the one who stayed.

military_service.notes: FA: officially registered as Captain and veteran of the Revolutionary War.

Died in the army at the defeat of Gen. Montgomery, Jul 24, 1776. This is likely the Battle of Trois-Rivières (June 1776) or a later skirmish during the Quebec campaign — "defeat of Gen. Montgomery" refers to the failed assault on Quebec City, Dec 31, 1775, though the death date 1776 suggests a subsequent action in the retreat from Canada.

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery, Hampton, Washington County, New York

narrative_blocks.header: She married a musician deacon in Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Clarissa Hooker left Connecticut and married Moses Ingersoll at Poultney, Vermont — a man the Ingersoll genealogy describes as a musician, choir director, and deacon. The family moved west, living first in Ashtabula County (Ohio's Western Reserve) and eventually in Augusta, Illinois, where Clarissa died in November 1851. She was 62.

burial.plot_notes: Old burial ground near Bristol-Plainville line, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: His sons turned the farm into factories
narrative_blocks.body: Asahel kept a large farm near the Bristol-Plainville line from about 1760, and with his sons took up manufacturing beside it — the seed of three generations of Hooker mills. He was buried in the old ground near the line he farmed.

orbit_connections.name: Joseph Harvey (Yale 1809) — succeeded Asahel at Goshen
orbit_connections.relationship: Harvey succeeded Asahel Hooker as pastor at Goshen CT in 1810
orbit_connections.notes: Yale Biographical Sketches (Dexter): "The Rev. Asahel Hooker (Yale 1789) was dismissed from his pastorate over the Congregational Church of Goshen, in Litchfield County, on June 12, 1810; and Mr. Harvey thereafter supplied the pulpit with such favor that he was unanimously called to the vacant place." Also: Joseph Edwards (Yale, graduated c.1806) "began the study of theology upon graduation with the Rev. Asahel Hooker (Yale 1789), of Goshen, at the same time conducting an academy there." Asahel Hooker's Goshen pastorate was evidently a center of theological training in Litchfield County during the early 19th century. (Yale Biographical Sketches of Grads 1805-1815, Dexter 1912; URL: https://www.cga.ct.gov/hco/books/Biographical_Sketches_of_Grads_of_Yale_1805-1815_1912.pdf)

notable.notable_blurb: pastor of the church in Goshen until dismissed

narrative_blocks.header: Eighteen years at Goshen, dismissed for ill health
narrative_blocks.body: Asahel Hooker was ordained at Goshen, Connecticut on September 7, 1791, and served the congregation as beloved pastor for eighteen years. He was dismissed June 12, 1810 — not over doctrine or congregational conflict, but because his health had broken. Throughout his Goshen tenure he was a published voice in the Connecticut Evangelical Magazine.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Jonathan Edwards's granddaughter Phebe Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: On June 11, 1792 he married Phebe Edwards (H00940), daughter of Hon. Timothy Edwards and granddaughter of Rev. Jonathan Edwards. Like Asahel, Phebe descended from Thomas Hooker — but through Mary Hooker Pierpont's line rather than the surname line. Their son Edward William Hooker would later write the 1849 biography of Thomas Hooker cited as the foundational source by Edward Hooker's 1909 Descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: His son wrote the 1849 Thomas Hooker biography
narrative_blocks.body: His son Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) wrote The Life of Rev. Thomas Hooker in 1849. The biography became the foundational source for nineteenth-century Hooker scholarship and was cited as a primary reference in Edward Hooker's 1909 Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker. Edward William inherited the double Hooker descent — surname line through Asahel, Edwards line through his mother Phebe.

education.notes: — Yale College 1789. Son of Asahel Hooker (H00262). Congregational pastor at Goshen CT 1791-1810, Norwich CT 1812-1813. Yale Biographical Sketches mention him as the pastor at Goshen when Joseph Harvey (Yale 1809) succeeded him and when Joseph Edwards (Yale student) studied theology with him.

narrative_blocks.header: First Bristol woolen manufactory, burned 1903
narrative_blocks.body: Bryan Hooker established one of the first woolen manufactories in Connecticut at Bristol — his fulling mill long known locally as 'the old yellow shop, near the bridge on the corner of East and South Streets.' The building stood for over a century before it was destroyed by fire in 1903 — seventy-seven years after Hooker's death. The mill represented the small-scale industrial transition between colonial-era home-spinning and the later New England textile factory system.

narrative_blocks.header: Delegate to 1818 Connecticut Constitution convention
narrative_blocks.body: On July 4, 1818, Bryan Hooker was appointed delegate to the Hartford convention that framed the new Connecticut Constitution — the document that finally separated church and state in Connecticut and replaced the colonial-era Fundamental Orders. He had served Bristol as Town Clerk in 1806 and from 1811 through 1820, and as Bristol's representative in the Connecticut General Assembly across four sessions between 1812 and 1817. He voted on the conservative side and chaired several important committees during the convention.

narrative_blocks.header: The old yellow shop by the bridge
narrative_blocks.body: Bryan built one of the first woolen manufactories in Connecticut; his fulling mill at the corner of East and South Streets was known for generations as the old yellow shop. It outlived him by seventy-seven years, until fire took it in 1903.

narrative_blocks.header: “Wouldn’t you like to see your mother?”
narrative_blocks.body: His new stepdaughter, nine-year-old Rhoda Ann Fuller, boycotted the wedding and fled to her grandparents. On Esquire Hooker’s advice no one pursued her for a full year — then he drove his carriage over “on business,” asked the question, and she came home for good, staying until her own marriage to the mapmaker Samuel Augustus Mitchell.

burial.plot_notes: Old Westmoreland Cemetery, Westmoreland, Oneida County, New York
Old Westmoreland Cemetery, Westmoreland, Oneida County, New York

military_service.notes: Captain of Militia. Responded to the Danbury Alarm, 1777.

burial.plot_notes: Old Westmoreland Cemetery, Westmoreland, Oneida County, New York

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.

was his wife’s sister and Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth’s grandmother

Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

open_questions.question: Connection to Sir John Standley, officer in Cromwell's army
open_questions.notes: EH notes Sir John Standley was an officer in Cromwell's army and that the family was then an old one with no known connection to the famous English Stanley family. EH argues the Connecticut settlers spelled the name correctly as 'Standley' and the later standardization to 'Stanley' was a mistake. Research at Farmington Historical Society and English army records may confirm.
open_questions.action: Check Cromwell's army officer records for Sir John Standley; check Farmington Historical Society; compare with Stanley family pedigrees

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America as Descended from John, Timothy, and Thomas Stanley of Hartford, CT, 1636 (1887)

parents.father_research_notes: John Stanley, emigrant from England — settled Hartford 1636

political_offices.title: Deputy to General Court
political_offices.notes: Warren: 'almost continually for thirty-seven years — with few exceptions, the longest term of service known in the annals of the state'

notable.notable_blurb: Long-serving Farmington deputy and militia captain

narrative_blocks.header: He marched against the Pequots at age thirteen
narrative_blocks.body: John Stanley came to Hartford as a boy in his uncle's care. In 1637, only thirteen, he joined the colony's expedition against the Pequots, the year after the settlers arrived.

narrative_blocks.header: He served thirty-seven years as a deputy
narrative_blocks.body: From 1659 to 1696 Stanley represented Farmington at the General Court almost without a break, one of the longest terms of service in the colony's records.

narrative_blocks.header: He built the house that became Farmington's museum
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1665 Stanley built a home on the Farmington street for his second wife. Long known as the Stanley-Whitman House, it survives today as the town's museum.

narrative_blocks.header: He rose to captain in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: During King Philip's War Stanley served as a lieutenant and then captain, the rank that gave him the name by which he was always known, Capt. John Stanley.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the same day as his sister
narrative_blocks.body: On December 5, 1645, John Stanley married Sarah Scott at Hartford. The same day his sixteen-year-old sister Ruth married Isaac Moore, and both young couples settled together at Farmington.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He acquired the property where this house was later built.

military_service.notes: Warren: 'when a mere boy of thirteen, he went in the expedition against the Pequots, the very year after the settlers came to Hartford'
Lieutenant then Captain; received 120-acre land grant from General Court 1674 and another 1687

bio.title: Captain
bio.notes: Surname also spelled 'Stanley' — EH argues 'Standley' is the correct original spelling

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT; gravestone partly illegible. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Stanley Genealogy.

bio.bio_blurb: Second wife of Capt. John Stanley; mother of Abigail Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband built her the Stanley-Whitman House
narrative_blocks.body: About 1665 Capt. John Stanley built a home on the Farmington street for his second wife, Sarah. She raised his younger children there, among them Abigail, who married Hon. John Hooker.
